skill.name: ask-matt
skill.description: Routes users to the right Matt Pocock skill or workflow for planning, prototyping, implementation, triage, and handoff work.
skill.summary: Choose the right Matt Pocock workflow for a coding task.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged inline command names, one documentation URL, and weak-crypto-like text in SKILL.md. Review found only markdown guidance, skill names, file names, and a reference link, with no executable code, network call, prompt injection, or secret handling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Inline Skill Command References
low_findings.description: The external command findings are markdown references to skill names such as /handoff, /prototype, /implement, and /compact. They do not execute shell commands and no command arguments are constructed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside markdown prose and list items. There is no Ruby code, shell invocation, script block, or executable file in the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a markdown hyperlink to explanatory documentation about context limits. The skill does not fetch the URL or transmit data to it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is only embedded in markdown text. No network API, browser automation, or command execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary words in the description and standalone guidance. No hashing, encryption, credential storage, or cryptographic implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are natural-language metadata and workflow documentation. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage.

content.value_statement: Engineering work often stalls when users choose the wrong planning or implementation path. This skill routes Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users to the right workflow.
actual_capabilities: Maps an engineering situation to the recommended Matt Pocock skill.
Explains the main idea-to-ship workflow from discovery to implementation.
Distinguishes when to use prototyping, handoff, triage, and implementation flows.
Gives context-window guidance for multi-session coding work.
Identifies setup steps required before first use of the related workflows.
limitations: It is a routing guide, not an implementation skill.
It assumes the related Matt Pocock skills are installed and available.
It does not inspect code or create project files by itself.
It does not replace human judgment for project scope or priority.

use_cases.title: Start a New Feature
use_cases.description: Select the right discovery, planning, and implementation path for a new coding idea.

use_cases.title: Split Large Work
use_cases.description: Decide when to turn a discussion into a PRD and issue list for multiple sessions.

use_cases.title: Handle Incoming Bugs
use_cases.description: Route raw bug reports and feature requests into triage before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Starting Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner routing for an unclear task.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a coding task but I do not know which Matt Pocock skill to use. Ask only what you need, then route me.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Between Planning and Implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Decide whether a task is ready to build.
prompt_templates.prompt: Here is my feature idea. Tell me whether I should start with grilling, a PRD, a prototype, or implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Session Build
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare larger work across context windows.
prompt_templates.prompt: This project will take several sessions. Recommend the Matt Pocock workflow from initial discussion through issue implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Audit My Current Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced routing for an active project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current engineering flow. Identify where I should hand off, compact, prototype, triage, or move to implementation.

output_examples.input: I have a rough product idea and an existing codebase.
output_examples.output: Start with grill-with-docs. Keep the discussion in one context window, then move to a PRD and issues if the build spans sessions.

output_examples.input: I need to test a user interface decision before committing to the design.
output_examples.output: Use handoff into a prototype session. Bring the result back with another handoff before continuing the main planning thread.

output_examples.input: Several user bug reports are waiting in the tracker.
output_examples.output: Use triage first. Convert raw reports into agent-ready issues, then use implement on each selected issue.

best_practices: Describe your current phase, available codebase, and desired outcome before asking for routing.
Use handoff when changing sessions but still needing the full current context.
Run setup before relying on the broader Matt Pocock engineering workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to implement code directly.
Do not compact during a planning phase that still depends on exact prior discussion.
Do not triage issues that were already produced by the PRD-to-issues flow.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It recommends which Matt Pocock skill or workflow to use for a specific engineering situation.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. It is markdown guidance and does not contain executable scripts or command runners.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when you are unsure whether to grill, prototype, hand off, triage, plan, or implement.

faq.question: Can it work without the other skills?
faq.answer: It can explain the workflow, but its practical value depends on the referenced skills being available.

faq.question: Is it for Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: It is written as a general routing guide for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Does it store project context?
faq.answer: No. It only explains when other skills should preserve or transfer context.
